coredata.dc:title: Boosting the performance of alkaline Al-air batteries by interfacial reconstruction via N-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Lei
Huang, Yue
Su, Ankang
Shang, Ting
Qu, Minghe
Shi, Wei
Qiang, Yujie
Marzouki, Riadh
coredata.dc:description: Al-air batteries (AABs) have garnered attention as an innovative energy source with remarkable performance. Unfortunately, the self-corrosion of Al anodes in alkaline electrolytes impedes its commercialization. This study presents N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), synthesized via the hydrothermal method, as electrolyte additives in a 4 M NaOH solution, owing to their quantum size, exceptional dispersion, and polar surface functional groups, which facilitate corrosion inhibition and interfacial stabilization. The influence of varying concentrations of N-CQDs on the self-corrosion of Al and the discharge characteristics of AABs was investigated. The reconstruction of Al-electrolyte interface was explored using electrochemical procedures, hydrogen evolution tests, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and molecular simulation methods. Our results indicated that at a concentration of 150 mg/L of the N-CQDs inhibitor, the inhibition efficiency reaches 68.4 %. Concurrently, the anode utilization improves from 41.4 % to 85.0 %, the capacity density escalates from 1234.6 to 2531.6 mAh g−1, and the energy density increases from 1540.2 to 3367.0 Wh kg−1. This concept of a dynamic monolayer protective interface presents an alluring avenue for stable Al anodes, as well as an effective strategy to advance the practical implementations of AABs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Al-air battery
Self-corrosion
N-CQDs
Electrolyte additive
Interface engineering

This work was also supported by the Foundation of the Department of Science and Technology of Guizhou Province (No. QKHJCZK[2022]555 ), the Project of Tongren Science and Technology Bureau (No. [2024]15 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22062022 ), and the Foundation of the Department of Education of Guizhou Province (Nos. QJJ[2022]003 , QJJ[2023]026 ), the Innovation Fund Project for Graduate Student of Tongren University (No. trxyyc-202417 ), and the Student's Platform for Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (No. 202210665075 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comparison of the capacity, earth abundance, and cost of various metal-anodes.
Characterizations of N-CQDs. The XPS high resolution (a) O 1s, (b) N 1s, and (c) C 1s spectra; (d) FT-IR, (e) Raman, (f) XRD, (g) UV–vis spectra, (h) TG curve, and (i) TEM images.
(a) PDP curves, (b) Nyquist diagrams, (c) Bode plots, and (d) electrochemical stability windows of Al samples in 4 M NaOH solution with and without N-CQDs inhibitor.
The equivalent circuit for analyzing EIS data.
(a) Hydrogen evolution volume, (b) hydrogen evolution rate and inhibition efficiency, and (c) Langmuir adsorption isotherm.
SEM-EDS, AFM, and CAM results of the Al samples surface under different conditions: (a, d) as-polished, (b, e) blank, and (c, f) 4 M NaOH +150 mg/L N-CQDs.
(a–g) High-resolution XPS spectra for the surface of Al samples immersed in 4 M NaOH solution without and with adding 150 mg/L N-CQDs, (h) UV–vis spectra, and (i) ATR-FTIR spectra.

(a) Open-circuit voltage, (b) infrared thermographic image, (c) specific capacity curves, (d) polarization and power density plots, (e) on/off alternate cyclic discharge plots, (f) multi-step chronopotentiometric curves, (g) light bulbs illuminated through a transformer, (h) voltage after transformation, (i) comparison of discharge parameters.
(a) HOMO and LUMO distributions of N-CQDs inhibitor, (b) side and (c) top views of the equilibrium adsorption configuration of N-CQDs on Al(111) surface, (d) RDF curve.
Inhibition mechanism of N-CQDs on Al-air battery anode in alkaline solution.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-CQDs using L-tryptophan and tannic acid as precursors.
Polarization parameters of Al samples in the absence and presence of different concentrations of N-CQDs in 4 M NaOH solution.
Electrochemical impedance parameters of Al in 4 M NaOH electrolyte without and with N-CQDs additive.

head.ce:title: Boosting the performance of alkaline Al-air batteries by interfacial reconstruction via N-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: L. Guo, School of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Al-air batteries (AABs) have garnered attention as an innovative energy source with remarkable performance. Unfortunately, the self-corrosion of Al anodes in alkaline electrolytes impedes its commercialization. This study presents N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), synthesized via the hydrothermal method, as electrolyte additives in a 4 M NaOH solution, owing to their quantum size, exceptional dispersion, and polar surface functional groups, which facilitate corrosion inhibition and interfacial stabilization. The influence of varying concentrations of N-CQDs on the self-corrosion of Al and the discharge characteristics of AABs was investigated. The reconstruction of Al-electrolyte interface was explored using electrochemical procedures, hydrogen evolution tests, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and molecular simulation methods. Our results indicated that at a concentration of 150 mg/L of the N-CQDs inhibitor, the inhibition efficiency reaches 68.4 %. Concurrently, the anode utilization improves from 41.4 % to 85.0 %, the capacity density escalates from 1234.6 to 2531.6 mAh g−1, and the energy density increases from 1540.2 to 3367.0 Wh kg−1. This concept of a dynamic monolayer protective interface presents an alluring avenue for stable Al anodes, as well as an effective strategy to advance the practical implementations of AABs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Technological advancements and the fast growth of the global population have rendered it more challenging to meet the incessant need for energy supplies [1]. Research into new energy sources is being conducted progressively, particularly in the area of metal-air batteries. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , AABs have gained prominence in recent years due to their affordability, abundant availability, versatility, and high energy density [2,3]. They are now seen as a potential future alternative to renewable energy. AABs use Al as the anode and atmospheric oxygen as the cathode. An alkaline solution is conventionally employed as the electrolyte. Nevertheless, the water in these solutions induces corrosion of the Al surface via oxidation-reduction processes [4]. Corrosion is the primary factor dissuading the advancement of AABs, since it inhibits the complete utilization of the raw material, adversely impacting the consumption rate of the Al anode and diminishing battery performance [5]. The current primary remedies are the incorporation of additional elements into the Al alloy and the use of electrolyte additives to reduce corrosion [6]. The latter may enhance battery longevity at a reduced cost compared to the former, significantly reducing the emission of parasitic hydrogen, while the former necessitates more research efforts and larger capital investments. Moreover, studies have shown that cold rolling deformation of the microstructure of an Al anode influences the discharge performance of AABs [7].

The predominant electrolyte additions for alkaline AABs are synthetic compounds, ionic liquids, amino acid derivatives, and plant extracts [8–10]. Mixed inorganic and organic additives are often used to inhibit corrosion in AABs, due to the constraints of the separate components. For example, Wang et al. developed an advanced hybrid corrosion inhibitor including alkyl polyglucoside (APG) and potassium stannate (K2SnO3) to effectively reduce the self-discharge corrosion of the Al anode in AABs [11]; Tang et al. discovered that the combination of ellagic acid and sodium stannate additives significantly reduces the corrosion rate of the Al anode [12]. However, research in this area is still in its early stages, and further examination is needed.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-discrete spherical carbon nanoparticles measuring less than 10 nm [13]. They are environmentally sustainable materials with excellent optical properties, fluorescence, high water solubility, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [14]. The structural attributes of the surface functional groups enhance their interaction with metal substrates [15,16]. Reports have emerged about their use as electrolyte additions in zinc-ion batteries. Wang et al. suggested using N, S-doped CQDs as an additive to produce uniform Zn deposition in order to reduce the interfacial concentration gradient [17]. The performance of Zn anodes was improved, attaining favorable rate performance at 20 mA cm−2 over 6000 cycles. By adding carbon dot additions to the mixed electrolyte, Zhang and co-workers successfully improved the electrode-electrolyte interface, increasing the stability of zinc anodes [18]. The longevity of Zn||Zn symmetric batteries extends to 4000 h with consistent voltage fluctuations, owing to the synergistic interaction between quantum dots and sulfonate ions. Xiong and colleagues developed nitrogen-doped and sulfonated carbon dots as multifunctional additives for cost-effective aqueous ZnSO4 electrolyte, with remarkable cycle life (4000 h) and coulombic efficiency (99.5 %) [19]. The use of CQDs in the anti-corrosion treatment of diverse metal surfaces is currently being examined; nevertheless, there is no verified evidence to justify the claim that CQDs may enhance the performance of an Al-air battery.

This study used L-tryptophan and tannic acid as precursors to synthesize N-CQDs, anticipated to serve as high-quality electrolyte additives for the stabilization of alkaline AABs. To enhance understanding of the adhesion mechanism of the additive to the metal substrate at the microscopic level, molecular modeling approaches using density functional theory (DFT) and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations have been used in the investigation of additive action processes. This work presents an effective and promising approach to tackle the challenges associated with the Al anode, and it has significant practical application potential.

Analytical-grade L-tryptophan, tannic acid, NaOH, and absolute alcohol were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All the chemicals were used as obtained without further purification. The Al samples were cut in block form with dimensions 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm; the electrode block was coated with epoxy resin adhesive, leaving a 1 cm2 exposed surface for further studies. Prior to each experiment, the Al specimens were sequentially treated with different grits of sandpaper. Subsequently, they were cleansed with deionized water, degreased with absolute alcohol, and dried before to each use.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the hydrothermal approach was used to synthesize N-CQDs. L-tryptophan (2.25 g, 11 mmol) and tannic acid (0.64 g, 375 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water with stirring. The dissolved mixture was thereafter placed in an autoclave and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. The combination was then cooled to ambient temperature. The N-CQDs solution was filtered via 0.45 μm and 0.22 μm microporous membranes to remove the majority of contaminants, followed by dialysis using a 500 Da bag in deionized water for 72 h. Ultimately, a solid powder was generated by a freeze-drying procedure.

The functional groups of N-CQDs were identified using an IRAffinity-1 Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The elemental composition and chemical bonding states were determined via XPS (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The crystal structure was measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Japan) spectroscopy. The morphology was observed with a JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan), and particle size distribution was assessed using Nano Measure software. The Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption characteristics of the N-CQDs were measured using a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Puxi General Instrument, China). The thermal gravimetric (TG) examination was conducted using a STA449F3 simultaneous thermal analyzer (NETZSCH, Germany) within a temperature range of 25 to 800 °C and a heating rate of 10 °C/min in an inert atmosphere.

Electrochemical experiments were performed using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd.) at room temperature. The corrosive medium was a 4 M NaOH solution with varying concentrations of N-CQDs. The Al specimen served as the working electrode, the platinum sheet electrode functioned as the counter electrode, and the Hg/HgO electrode acted as the reference electrode. The open circuit potential (OCP) of the Al sample was constantly observed for a designated duration. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were conducted by applying an alternating perturbation throughout a frequency spectrum of 1 to 104 Hz with an amplitude signal of ±10 mV. The EIS results were modelled using ZSimpWin 3.6 software. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurements were executed immediately after EIS by varying the working electrode potential from −250 to +250 mV relative to E ocp at a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1. To guarantee the repeatability of the findings, each test was conducted thrice.

The evolution of hydrogen was investigated with apparatus including a conical flask, square groove, graduated burette, and gas supply tube (see Fig. S1). The gas obtained via a drainage technique measures the hydrogen generated and signifies the corrosion rate. The exposure area of the Al samples was 1 cm2, and data were recorded every 5 min for a duration of 0.5 h at room temperature. Utilizing the acquired data, the hydrogen evolution rate (S H2) and inhibition efficiency (η H2) were calculated using the following formulae [20,21]:
(1)
S
H 2
=
V
A × t
(2)
η
H 2
%
=
S 0
−
S inh
S 0
× 100
where V denotes the volume of hydrogen evolution, A signifies the exposure area of the sample, and t indicates the immersion duration. S 0 and S inh represent the corrosion rates of the blank solution and the solution containing the N-CQDs inhibitor, respectively.

The Al sample was polished using sandpaper with a particle size ranging from 120 to 1200, followed by rinsing in deionized water and absolute alcohol for 1 min. Subsequent to immersing the specimen in diverse solution medium for 1 h, it was washed with deionized water and dried in an oven for 10 min. SEM (JEOL, JSM-7800F, Japan) and AFM (MFP-3D-BIO, Asylum Research, USA) were used to assess alterations in the surface morphology and roughness of the Al metal after immersion in both pristine and inhibited electrolytes. Contact angle measurements (CAM, JY-PHa) were performed to investigate the hydrophobicity of the desiccated Al samples. XPS was used to examine the elemental composition of the surface after the soaking experiment.

The AABs system consisted of a custom discharge cell with an Al anode, an air cathode, and an electrolyte (Fig. S2). The performance of the battery was assessed utilizing a circulatory pump system, wherein an identical volume of solution was introduced into the storage vessel on each occasion, while sustaining a flow rate of 5 mL min−1. The air cathode was fabricated in our laboratory, including a nickel mesh, activated carbon, manganese oxide-based electrocatalytic components, and a binder (more details can be found in Fig. S3). Galvanostatic discharge studies were conducted at a current density of 20 mA cm−2 for a duration of 1 h. The intermittent discharge tests used 1 h of galvanostatic discharge, followed by 20 min of open circuit potential, repeated cyclically for four iterations. Linear sweep voltammetry was employed to obtain the polarization curves and power density profiles at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. Multi-current step tests were performed to assess the stability of batteries discharged at continually varying current densities ranging from 1 to 120 mA cm−2. The battery's performance was evaluated concurrently using the following equations [22]:
(3)
Q =
It Δm
(4)
U a
%
=
9 It
ΔmF
× 100
(5)
W =
EIt Δm
where Q and U a represent the capacity density and utilization of the Al anode, t signifies the discharge period, I stands for current, and ∆m indicates the weight loss. W denotes real energy density, whereas E represents average discharge voltage.

DFT calculations were conducted to investigate the molecular activity of the N-CQDs inhibitor using the DMol3 code incorporated in BIOVIA Materials Studio program [23]. The optimization computations used the double numerical polarization basis set and the BLYP exchange-correlation functional. The convergence requirements of energy, maximum force tolerance, and maximum displacement were established at 1 × 10−5 Ha, 2 × 10−3 Ha Å−1, and 5 × 10−3 Å, respectively. Thereafter, MD simulations were used to investigate the adsorption characteristics of N-CQDs on the Al substrate using the Forcite module of Materials Studio. The liquid flow was simulated using the confined shear approach. The surface of Al(111) was chosen as a representation of the metal substrate because to its multiple coordination sites, wide facet area, and high thermal stability [24]. The simulation box was constructed with dimensions 54.4 × 54.4 × 32.0 Å3 using periodic boundary conditions. It included a bottom Al(111) layer submerged in a fluid comprising 2500 H2O molecules and one N-CQDs molecule. The top wall was designated as the top 3 Å-thick H2O layer, whereas the Al(111) plane was chosen as the bottom wall. The top and bottom walls moved in opposing directions, with a wall velocity established at 0.03 Å/ps. The whole system was operated at 298 K with the COMPASSIII force field, Andersen thermostat, NVT canonical ensemble, with a time step of 1.0 fs, throughout a simulation duration of 3 ns. Electrostatic interactions were addressed using the Ewald approach, whereas van der Waals interactions were managed by atom-based methods.

XPS analysis identified the three components of N-CQDs: C, N, and O atoms. The relative spectra shown in Fig. 2a–c were acquired by the Gaussian fitting approach. The O 1 s peak corresponds to H − O, C − O − H, C − O, and CO bonds with energies of 530.8, 531.5, 532.5, and 533.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 2a) [25]. Two N 1s peaks correspond to C − N and N − H bonds with energies of 399.2 and 400.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b) [26]. The C 1s peak is associated with C − C/C=C/C − H, C − O − C/C − N, and O − C=O bonds, exhibiting energies of 284.5, 285.6, and 288.1, respectively (Fig. 2c) [27]. In the FT-IR spectrum seen in Fig. 2d, the distinct peaks at 3396 and 3062 cm−1 are attributed to the vibrational stretching of −OH and benzene C − H bonds, respectively [28]. The peaks at 1587 and 1411 cm−1 represent the amide band of the −CONH− bond and the shear vibration of −CH2 group, respectively [29]. The peak at 744 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrational bending occurring outside the −CH plane of the benzene ring [30]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 2e) shows a peak at 3067 cm−1 corresponding to the CH group, and another at 3255 cm−1 associated with the NH group of the amino acid [31]. Fig. 2f depicts the XRD pattern of N-CQDs. The peak at 2θ = 21.8° corresponds to the carbon moiety identified in graphene-like structures [32]. The broad diffraction peak indicates that the N-CQDs material is inherently amorphous, influenced by carbonaceous groups or doped heteroelements. The UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs seen in Fig. 2g exhibits three absorption peaks at 199, 218, and 278 nm, attributed to charge transfer transitions of the conjugated system (benzene ring), the presence of peptide groups (−CO − NH− bonds), and aromatic amino acid residues (−NH2) within the structure [33]. The fluorescence characteristics of N-CQDs are shown by the transformation of the aqueous solution to light yellow upon exposure to visible light, and to blue upon excitation at 365 nm. The TG result shown in Fig. 2h indicates that the breakdown of N-CQDs transpires between 250–300 °C, confirming the electrolyte's stability throughout the Al-air battery reaction process. N-CQDs have quasi-spherical shapes, elevated dispersity, and absence of aggregation, with size distributions adhering to a Gaussian pattern (mean size of 8.8 nm), as seen in the TEM images of Fig. 2i.

Fig. 3a shows the PDP curves of the Al electrode in 4 M NaOH corrosive environment with varying N-CQD concentrations. The anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (β a and β c), corrosion potential (E corr), and corrosion current density (i corr) were determined using the conventional Tafel extrapolation technique. The inhibition efficiency (η PDP) was calculated as [34]:
(6)
η PDP
%
=
i
corr , 0
−
i
corr , inh
i
corr , 0
× 100
where i corr,0 and i corr,inh are the corrosion current density without and with the N-CQDs inhibitor, respectively. The acquired polarization parameters are shown in Table 1 . The PDP curves change towards areas of low corrosion current density with the introduction of varying amounts of N-CQDs. Consequently, the i corr value significantly diminishes, inhibiting corrosion on the Al surface. The electrode potential of the Al anode in the pristine solution is −1.471 V (vs. Hg/HgO); the incorporation of N-CQDs induces a positive change in the corrosion potential. Upon the incorporation of 150 mg/L N-CQDs, the value transitions to −1.451 V. Nonetheless, the total voltage variation is below 85 mV, indicating that N-CQDs function as mixed-type inhibitors on both the anode and cathode [35]. The efficacy of the inhibition progressively escalates with the augmentation of N-CQDs concentration. At a concentration of 150 mg/L of N-CQDs, the inhibitory effectiveness attains 68.4 %. Table 1 additionally presents the conductivity of the electrolyte both in the absence and presence of N-CQDs. The inclusion of N-CQDs leads to a little reduction in conductivity. Decreasing the concentration of conductive ions in the water participating in the self-corrosion reaction may significantly reduce the corrosion rate.

Fig. 3b illustrates the Nyquist plots of the Al anode in the concerned electrolyte systems. The observed curves exhibit a comparable shape, suggesting that the Al anode's response in the electrolyte is mostly impervious to the N-CQDs additive. The gradual enlargement of the diameter of the Nyquist plots corresponding to the increasing concentration of N-CQDs signifies an enhancement in the surface adsorption of the additive, which in turn diminishes the charge transfer. The loss of one electron transforms Al into Al+, establishing a capacitive circuit in the high-frequency domain; an oxidation reaction transforms Al+ into Al3+, altering the capacitance circuit in the low-frequency range; the inductance circuit in the mid-frequency band is ascribed to the adsorption and accumulation of corrosion intermediates [36]. The aforementioned process may be shown by the following reactions [37]:
(7)
Al +
OH −
→ Al
OH
ads
+
e −
(8)
Al
OH
ads
+
OH −
→ Al
OH
2 , ads
+
e −
(9)
Al
OH
2 , ads
+
OH −
→ Al
OH
3 , ads
+
e −
(10)
Al
OH
3 , ads
+
OH −
→ Al
OH
4 , ads
−

The Bode plot depicted in Fig. 3c illustrates the phase angle constants at varying concentrations of N-CQDs, revealing two distinct peaks that correspond to the dual capacitance structure identified during the reaction process. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the augmentation of the N-CQDs concentration broadens the electrochemical stability window. Specifically, the reduction current density transitions from −87.0 to −48.5 mA cm−2 upon the incorporation of 150 mg/L N-CQDs into the 4 M NaOH solution, resulting in a differential of −38.5 mA cm−2. This variation demonstrated that an increase in the concentration of the additive resulted in the Al electrode maintaining stability throughout a broader potential range.

Fig. 4 illustrates a model of an equivalent circuit that is beneficial for elucidating impedance data. It includes solution resistance (R s), constant phase elements (CPE1, CPE2), charge transfer resistance (R ct,1, R ct,2), uncompensated resistance (R), and inductance (L). The polarization resistance (R p) is determined utilizing the subsequent equation [38]:
(11)
R p
=
R
ct , 1
× R
R
ct , 1
+ R
+
R
ct , 2

The impedance parameters derived from the simulated Nyquist diagrams are shown in Table 2 . The chi-square value (χ 2) values vary from 1.05 × 10−4 to 4.64 × 10−4, indicating the precision of fitting the EIS data with the chosen equivalent circuit. With the addition of N-CQDs and the formation of the corrosion-resistant film on the Al electrode, R ct,1, R ct,2, and R p all rise concurrently when the N-CQD concentrations vary in the 4 M NaOH electrolyte. The incorporation of N-CQD may alter the configuration of the Al-electrolyte interface, increase the thickness of the barrier layer, or reduce the local dielectric constant, so hindering charge transfer between the Al anode and the solution [39]. The L value consistently rises with the elevation of inhibitor concentration, indicating that the additive is perpetually adsorbing and displacing the solvent molecules.

The findings indicate that the incorporation of the N-CQDs inhibitor diminishes the hydrogen output from the alkaline solution (refer to Fig. 5a). The gas evolution rate from the Al anode in the blank electrolyte is 0.92 mL min−1 cm−2, which decreases to 0.30 mL min−1 cm−2 with the introduction of a 150 mg/L N-CQDs additive (Fig. 5b). The inhibition effectiveness attains 67 %. The protective layer created by the adsorption of N-CQDs on the Al surface impedes the corrosion process. The data from the hydrogen evolution experiment was used to analyze the adsorption isotherm, assessing the behavior of the N-CQDs inhibitor on the Al surface. Consequently, the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, which was produced by fitting the data to a linear correlation coefficient value (R 2 = 0.9905, Fig. 5c), may be explained by the following equation after surface coverage (θ) was determined [40]:
(12)
θ
1 − θ
=
K ads
C add

In this context, θ represents the surface coverage (θ = η
H2), C add denotes the concentration of N-CQDs additive, and K ads signifies the adsorption equilibrium constant. The free energy of the adsorption (ΔG0 ads) process is ultimately characterized by the equation [41]:
(13)
Δ
G ads 0
= − R T ln
1000
K ads
where R is the universal gas constant, T the absolute temperature, and 1000 (g/L) the molar concentration of water in solution. The ΔG0 ads value for the adsorption of the N-CQDs additive on the Al surface is −27.28 kJ/mol, which falls within the range of −20 to −40 kJ/mol, demonstrating the spontaneous character of the process, including both physical and chemical adsorption [42].

The formation of a monolayer adsorption deposit on the Al surface that reduces the rate of corrosion of the metal can be further examined through microscopy analysis. SEM and AFM methods were used to observe changes in the surface topography of Al following immersion in a 4 M NaOH solution, both with and without 150 mg/L of N-CQDs for 1 h. In Fig. 6a, the bare Al surface exhibits a rough texture while retaining the original elemental makeup of the polished metal. The introduction of 4 M NaOH solution compromises the morphology of the previously smooth Al surface, resulting in the formation of many corrosion pits on the substrate (see Fig. 6b). The shielding layer seen in Fig. 6c is created when the additional N-CQDs inhibitor adsorbs onto the Al surface, slowing down corrosion.

The EDS data indicate that the chemical composition of the Al surface varies depending on the immersion medium. When the electrode is immersed in a 4 M NaOH solution, the water molecules chemically interact with Al atoms, leading to a decrease in the original Al concentration (93.74 %). Upon the addition of N-CQDs, the protective film that develops on the Al surface diminishes the direct contact between free water and the metal surface, leading to an increase in the Al component concentration (94.83 %). Additionally, the detection of C and N components corroborated the adsorption of N-CQDs on the Al surface. The AFM findings (Fig. 6d–f) indicate that the alkaline solution elevates the roughness from 121.5 nm to 217.3 nm and reduces the contact angle from 41.1° to 24.5°. Following the introduction of 150 mg/L N-CQDs inhibitor, the values altered to 189.4 nm and 34.9°, respectively. In Fig. S4, metallographic microscopy reveals the impacts of additive adsorption on the Al surface that results in the development of a shielding layer raising the hydrophobicity of Al.

High-resolution XPS spectra were examined to further the knowledge of the composition and distinctive bonding of the Al surface after immersion for 1 h in the blank solution (Fig. 7a–c) and in the electrolyte containing 150 mg/L of N-CQDs (Fig. 7d–g). The principal components of the surface immersed in the blank solution are the elements O, Al, and C. The O 1 s peak mostly consists of O − H and Al − O bonds with energies of 530.8 eV and 529.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 7a). In the protected samples, these two peaks shift to 531.3 and 529.5 eV, respectively (see Fig. 7d). After the addition of N-CQDs inhibitor, the Al(OH)3, Al − Al, and Al2O3 peaks in Fig. 7b shifted from 73.2, 72.5, and 70.9 eV to 73.5, 72.6, and 71.0 eV, respectively, as seen in Fig. 7e [43]. Moreover, in Fig. 7c, the C1s peaks at 284.2, 285.3, 287.2, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to C − C, CC, C − N, and O=C − O bonds, respectively, shift to 283.9, 285.4 and 287.2 eV after the incorporation of N-CQDs (Fig. 7f) [44]. The disappearance of the C 1s peak associated with the O=C − O bond from the blank to the suppressed sample is attributable to the protective layer that forms on the Al surface after the introduction of inhibitor. This layer obstructs interactions between Al atoms and water molecules in solution. In Fig. 7g, the N 1s peak at 398.3 eV corresponds to the = C − N bond in the inhibited scenario [45].

The UV–vis spectra in Fig. 7h show an absorption peak of the N-CQDs benzene ring at 196 nm, attributed to the π-π* charge transfer transition, and a peak at 278 nm, resulting from the presence of aromatic amino acid residues [46]. The relative intensities diminish after 1 h of immersion subsequent to the adsorption of N-CQDs on the Al surface. ATR-FTIR spectra of Al samples in 4 M NaOH, both with and without 150 mg/L of N-CQDs, were obtained to identify alterations in functional groups on the Al surface (Fig. 7i). The observations indicate a peak at 1361 cm−1 associated with the Al surface immersed in blank solution (blue line), in contrast to the polished substrate (red line), attributed to the presence of hydroxyl groups. This peak shifts to 1376 cm−1 with the addition of N-CQDs. Moreover, the study finds on the benzene ring at 2908–3630 cm−1 stretching vibrations of −NH/−OH and C − H bonds; the −NH bending vibration explains the peak at 1567 cm−1 [47].

The laboratory-constructed Al-air battery has an open circuit voltage of 1.929 V (Fig. 8a). Infrared thermal imaging pictures utilized to monitor temperature variations throughout the battery discharge process (Fig. 8b) demonstrate that the Al's surface achieves a maximum temperature of 24.4 °C and a low temperature of 19.9 °C (average temperature = 22.4 °C). Fig. 8c illustrates the capacity density curve, indicating that the incorporation of N-CQDs enhances the battery's voltage, as shown by the discharge curve exhibiting a significant upward shift in comparison to the control (refer to Fig. 8d). Furthermore, the power density is significantly enhanced, with the maximum value increasing from 40.2 to 55.5 mW cm−2. To evaluate the feasibility of the electrolyte, intermittent discharge experiments replicating the practical application of the Al-air battery were carried out (Fig. 8e).

The battery containing the N-CQDs additive exhibits a superior discharge voltage compared to the blank electrolyte. Additionally, the voltage decay was successfully alleviated. The multi-step chronopotentiometric curves (Fig. 8f) evaluated the discharge voltage at 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 mA cm−2. The voltage of the electrolyte with N-CQDs additive is much lower than that of the control condition, indicating enhanced discharge performance. When we use a transformer (Fig. 8g) to increase the voltage of the Al-air single cell, its voltage may easily approach 5 V (Fig. 8h), which can effectively operate certain tiny devices, such as the miniature crane in Supplementary Video S1.

The battery containing the N-CQDs additive exhibits a superior discharge voltage compared to the blank electrolyte. Additionally, the voltage decay was successfully alleviated. The multi-step chronopotentiometric curves (Fig. 8f) evaluated the discharge voltage at 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 mA cm−2. The voltage of the electrolyte with N-CQDs additive is much lower than that of the control condition, indicating enhanced discharge performance. When we use a transformer (Fig. 8g) to increase the voltage of the Al-air single cell, its voltage may easily approach 5 V (Fig. 8h), which can effectively operate certain tiny devices, such as the miniature crane in Supplementary Video S1.

We can sum up the battery's overall effectiveness both before and after adding 150 mg/L of N-CQDs as shown in Fig. 8i. The average discharge voltage increases from 1.247 V to 1.330 V at a current density of 20 mA cm−2; the anode utilization escalates from 41.4 % to 85 %; the battery's capacity density advances from 1234.6 to 2531.6 mAh g−1; the relative energy density amplifies from 1540.2 to 3367.0 Wh kg−1. Consequently, the results of the conducted experiments indicate that the performance of the Al-air battery is significantly improved by the incorporation of N-CQDs as an electrolyte additive. Table S1 illustrates its distinct excellence relative to other published work.

The reactivity of N-CQDs was analyzed using DFT calculations. Fig. 9a demonstrates that the HOMO and LUMO regions of N-CQDs mostly reside on the aromatic six-membered rings and are dispersed across the whole molecular framework, facilitating electron sharing with the Al surface to establish coordination bonds. To elucidate the adsorption mechanism of N-CQDs additive on the Al surface, MD simulation was conducted to validate this interaction process. The most favorable equilibrium arrangement is shown in Fig. 9b and c. To attain the highest surface coverage ability, the N-CQDs molecule is obviously strongly connected to the Al substrate and exhibits a parallel adsorption mode. Fig. 9d illustrates the radial distribution function (RDF) of the N-CQDs inhibitor, demonstrating that the bond length between Al and N-CQDs is under 3.5 Å, hence suggesting that chemisorption predominates in the examined adsorption system [48].

The strength of N-CQDs inhibitor adsorbed on the Al surface may be quantified by the adsorption energy (E ads), which can be assessed using the following equation [49]:
(14)
E ads
=
E total
−
E
surf + sol
+
E
add + sol
+
E sol
where E total represents the total potential energy of the system, including the Al surface, the adsorbed additive molecule, and the solution; E surf+sol and E add+sol are the potential energies of the system excluding the additive and the system devoid of the Al crystal, respectively; E sol is the potential energy associated with the solvent molecules. In general, a higher absolute magnitude of E ads correlates with enhanced adsorption capacity [50]. The E ads value for the N-CQDs additive was −230 kcal/mol under dynamic conditions. This indicates that the adsorption of N-CQDs occurs spontaneously.

In an alkaline electrolyte, the electrode reactions in an alkaline solution can be represented as follows [51]:
Anode:
(15)
Al + 4
OH −
→ Al
OH
4 −
+ 3
e −
− 2.33
V
vs . SHE
Cathode:
(16)
O 2
+ 2
H 2
O + 4
e −
→ 4
OH −
0.40
V
vs . SHE
Overall reaction:
(17)
4 Al + 3
O 2
+ 6
H 2
O + 4
OH −
→ 4 Al
OH
4 −
2.73
V
vs . SHE

The predicted voltage of 2.73 V has been empirically shown to be below 2 V due to corrosion and the formation of a passivating layer. The reduction of water and the phenomenon of self-corrosion may be comprehended as follows [52]:
(18)
2
H 2
O + 2
e −
→
H 2
+ 2
OH −
− 0.83
V
vs . SHE
(19)
4 Al + 6
H 2
O + 2
OH −
+ 2
e −
→ 3
H 2
+ 4 Al
OH
4 −
1.48
V
vs . SHE

The Al electrode undergoes corrosion due to the interaction of H2O molecules with the metal, promoting electron transfer from the Al anode to the cathode, hence diminishing anode utilization and electrochemical performance. Following the incorporation of the N-CQDs additive, the inhibitor adheres to the Al surface, leading to a reconfiguration of the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte. This process inhibits the interaction of Al with H2O molecules in the alkaline solution, therefore reducing metal surface corrosion (Fig. 10 ). Ultimately, the overall efficacy of the battery is enhanced.

This research indicates that AABs may substantially benefit from the incorporation of N-CQDs as an effective addition. The molecular structure of N-CQDs, characterized by several electron-rich sites including O and N atoms, as well as π-electrons from the benzene ring, promotes interactions with Al atoms, resulting in their spontaneous adsorption onto the metal surface. Surface analysis, adsorption isotherm, and molecular modeling findings together suggest that this adsorption occurs via a mix of chemisorption and physisorption processes. Remarkably, N-CQDs have an impressive inhibitory effectiveness of 68.4 % at a dosage of 150 mg/L. This results in a significant enhancement in the anode utilization of the battery, increasing from 41.4 % to 85 %. Additionally, the mass capacity and energy densities attain values of 2531.6 mAh g−1 and 3367.0 Wh kg−1, respectively. Thus, the corrosion of the Al anode is successfully mitigated, while the overall performance of the battery is markedly improved.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary Video S1 Supplementary Video S1
Supplementary material
Image 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.est.2025.117136.

Lei Guo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yue Huang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ankang Su: Visualization, Data curation. Ting Shang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Minghe Qu: Software, Methodology. Wei Shi: Supervision, Resources. Yujie Qiang: Validation, Methodology. Riadh Marzouki: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Software.
